name: limonene
notes: EU Annex III declarable fragrance allergen (Regulation 1223/2009, entry 68 as amended by Reg 2023/1545). Must be declared on cosmetic labels when ≥0.001% in leave-on products or ≥0.01% in rinse-off. Naturally present in citrus peel oils, many essential oils. SCCS/1659/21 (2022) confirmed allergenicity. Oxidised limonene (formed on air exposure) is a stronger sensitiser than the fresh compound. IARC Group 3 (unclassifiable) — no carcinogenicity concern.

name: linalool
notes: EU Annex III declarable fragrance allergen (Regulation 1223/2009, entry 69 as amended). Declaration required at ≥0.001% leave-on, ≥0.01% rinse-off. Found in lavender, coriander, basil, and hundreds of essential oils. One of the most widely used fragrance ingredients globally. SCCS/1659/21 (2022) confirmed sensitisation potential. Oxidised linalool is more allergenic than fresh. Not classified by IARC or Prop 65.

name: citral
notes: EU Annex III declarable fragrance allergen (Regulation 1223/2009, entry 55). Declaration required at ≥0.001% leave-on, ≥0.01% rinse-off. CAS 5392-40-5 is the technical mixture of geranial (CAS 141-27-5) and neral (CAS 106-26-3). Present in citrus oils, lemongrass, verbena. Known skin sensitiser and irritant at higher concentrations. SCCS/1659/21 (2022). Also used as a food flavouring; EFSA set ADI via flavouring re-evaluation.

name: eugenol
notes: EU Annex III declarable fragrance allergen (Regulation 1223/2009, entry 59). Declaration at ≥0.001% leave-on, ≥0.01% rinse-off. Major component of clove oil; also in cinnamon leaf, nutmeg, basil. Common cause of contact allergic dermatitis (patch test positive in ~1–2% of general population). SCCS/1659/21 (2022). IARC Group 3 — not classifiable as to carcinogenicity. Also classified as a fragrance allergen in food flavouring context (EFSA).

name: geraniol
notes: EU Annex III declarable fragrance allergen (Regulation 1223/2009, entry 61). Declaration at ≥0.001% leave-on, ≥0.01% rinse-off. Present in rose oil, geranium oil, citronella oil, and many essential oil blends. Moderate sensitisation potential; significant cross-reactivity with citronellol. SCCS/1659/21 (2022). Not listed by IARC or Prop 65.

name: cinnamal
notes: EU Annex III declarable fragrance allergen (Regulation 1223/2009, entry 54). Declaration at ≥0.001% leave-on, ≥0.01% rinse-off. Higher score_penalty (10) than most allergens: cinnamal is one of the most potent fragrance sensitisers; positive patch test rate up to 2–3% in dermatology clinic populations. Found in cinnamon bark oil, cassia oil. Can also cause immediate contact urticaria (type I) in addition to delayed sensitisation. SCCS/1659/21 (2022) confirmed strong sensitisation potential.

name: benzyl alcohol
notes: EU Annex III declarable fragrance allergen (Regulation 1223/2009, entry 51). Declaration at ≥0.001% leave-on, ≥0.01% rinse-off. Also used as a preservative in cosmetics (max 1.0% under EU Annex V). Present in jasmine, ylang ylang, hyacinth oils. Dual function: fragrance ingredient and antimicrobial preservative. SCCS/1659/21 (2022). Sensitisation potential is relatively low among the 26 allergens but combined preservative/fragrance exposure is relevant.

name: benzyl benzoate
notes: EU Annex III declarable fragrance allergen (Regulation 1223/2009, entry 52). Declaration at ≥0.001% leave-on, ≥0.01% rinse-off. Naturally present in balsams (Balsam of Peru, tolu balsam), jasmine, ylang ylang. Also used pharmaceutically as a scabicide/antiparasitic. SCCS/1659/21 (2022). Cross-reactive with other balsam components; patients with balsam-of-Peru allergy typically react. Prop 65 not listed; IARC not classified.

name: benzyl salicylate
notes: EU Annex III declarable fragrance allergen (Regulation 1223/2009, entry 53). Declaration at ≥0.001% leave-on, ≥0.01% rinse-off. Widely used as a UV absorber and fixative in fragrances; imparts floral note. Found in ylang ylang, carnation. SCCS/1659/21 (2022) confirmed allergenicity. Additionally acts as a mild UV filter — this dual function makes it common in both fragranced and sun-protection cosmetics.

name: coumarin
notes: EU Annex III declarable fragrance allergen (Regulation 1223/2009, entry 56). Declaration at ≥0.001% leave-on, ≥0.01% rinse-off. Higher score_penalty (10): coumarin is also restricted as a food flavouring (EU Reg 1334/2008 max 2 mg/kg in most foods, 10 mg/kg in drinks) due to hepatotoxicity at high dietary intake. EFSA 2004 and 2008 opinions on coumarin in food. Present naturally in tonka beans, cassia cinnamon, sweet woodruff. SCCS/1659/21 (2022) confirmed cosmetic allergenicity. IARC Group 3 — not classifiable as to carcinogenicity.

name: isoeugenol
notes: EU Annex III declarable fragrance allergen (Regulation 1223/2009, entry 64). Declaration at ≥0.001% leave-on, ≥0.01% rinse-off. Higher score_penalty (10): isoeugenol is among the stronger sensitisers in the 26-allergen list; patch-test positive rates are higher than for most others. Prohibited from use in perfumes in some markets. Found in ylang ylang, carnation, geranium. SCCS/1659/21 (2022) and SCCS/1541/14 specifically addressed isoeugenol restriction. Structural isomer of eugenol but considerably more allergenic.

name: farnesol
notes: EU Annex III declarable fragrance allergen (Regulation 1223/2009, entry 60). Declaration at ≥0.001% leave-on, ≥0.01% rinse-off. Sesquiterpene alcohol; present in rose, lily-of-the-valley, linden blossom oils. Also a natural component of many plant essential oils used in 'natural' cosmetics. SCCS/1659/21 (2022) confirmed allergenicity. Not in IARC or Prop 65.

name: citronellol
notes: EU Annex III declarable fragrance allergen (Regulation 1223/2009, entry 57). Declaration at ≥0.001% leave-on, ≥0.01% rinse-off. Present in rose oil, geranium oil, citronella oil. Cross-reactivity with geraniol is common. Widely used in household and personal care fragrances. SCCS/1659/21 (2022). Not in IARC or Prop 65.

name: hexyl cinnamaladehyde
notes: EU Annex III declarable fragrance allergen (Regulation 1223/2009, entry 63). Declaration at ≥0.001% leave-on, ≥0.01% rinse-off. One of the most widely used synthetic fragrance ingredients, imparting a jasmine-chamomile note. Not naturally occurring — synthetic. SCCS/1659/21 (2022) confirmed sensitisation potential. Among the more frequently detected allergens in patch-test studies of cosmetic-related contact dermatitis. Not in IARC or Prop 65.

name: alpha-isomethyl ionone
notes: EU Annex III declarable fragrance allergen (Regulation 1223/2009, entry 50). Declaration at ≥0.001% leave-on, ≥0.01% rinse-off. Synthetic fragrance ingredient with a violet/orris/woody note. Widely used in fine fragrances, hair care, and fabric softeners. SCCS/1659/21 (2022) confirmed allergenicity. Not in IARC or Prop 65.
